class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Statistics and Descriptive Analysis
learning_objectives: Distinguish between descriptive and inferential statistics
Calculate measures of central tendency and dispersion
Create and interpret histograms, box plots, and scatter plots
readings: Anderson et al., Statistics for Business & Economics Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Statistics is the science of collecting, analyzing, and interpreting data to make informed decisions. In business, data-driven decisions consistently outperform gut instinct.
key_concepts: Population vs. sample; parameter vs. statistic
Measures of center: mean, median, mode
Measures of spread: range, variance, standard deviation, IQR
Distribution shape: skewness and kurtosis
Data visualization: histograms, box plots, scatter plots
lecture_notes.demonstration: Descriptive analysis of a real business dataset: calculating summary statistics, creating visualizations, and identifying patterns and outliers
setup_requirements: Textbook: Anderson et al., Statistics for Business & Economics (14th ed.)
Statistical software (Excel with Data Analysis Toolpak, or R/Python)

review.value: Average distance of data points from the mean; the most commonly used measure of variability. Lower SD = less spread, more consistency.
Shows median, Q1, Q3, IQR, and outliers in one visualization; excellent for comparing distributions across groups

learning_objectives: Apply basic probability rules: addition, multiplication, complement
Calculate conditional probabilities and apply Bayes' theorem
Understand discrete and continuous probability distributions
readings: Anderson et al., Statistics for Business & Economics Ch. 4-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Probability is the mathematical foundation of statistical inference. Understanding probability enables us to quantify uncertainty and make rational decisions under risk.
key_concepts: Sample space, events, and probability axioms
Addition rule: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)
Multiplication rule: P(A and B) = P(A) x P(B|A)
Independence vs. dependence
Bayes' theorem: updating probabilities with new information
lecture_notes.demonstration: Bayes' theorem application: calculating the probability that a positive drug test indicates actual drug use given test accuracy rates (false positive/negative problem)

review.value: P(A|B) = P(B|A) x P(A) / P(B); allows us to update our belief about event A after observing evidence B
P(A|B): the probability of A given that B has occurred; fundamentally different from P(A and B)

learning_objectives: Apply the normal distribution to calculate probabilities
Explain the Central Limit Theorem and its importance
Calculate probabilities using the sampling distribution of the mean
readings: Anderson et al., Statistics for Business & Economics Ch. 6-7

lecture_notes.introduction: The normal distribution and the Central Limit Theorem are the foundations of statistical inference. They explain why we can make reliable conclusions about populations from samples.
key_concepts: Normal distribution: bell-shaped, symmetric, defined by mean and standard deviation
Z-score = (X - mu) / sigma; standardizes any normal to standard normal
Empirical rule: 68-95-99.7% within 1, 2, 3 standard deviations
Central Limit Theorem: sample means are normally distributed regardless of population shape
Standard error of the mean = sigma / sqrt(n)
lecture_notes.demonstration: CLT simulation: drawing repeated samples from a skewed population and showing that the distribution of sample means becomes normal as sample size increases

review.value: For large enough samples (n >= 30), the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal with mean = population mean and SE = sigma/sqrt(n)
The standard deviation of the sampling distribution; decreases with larger samples, meaning larger samples give more precise estimates

learning_objectives: Construct and interpret confidence intervals for means and proportions
Determine required sample size for desired precision
Understand the tradeoff between confidence level and interval width
readings: Anderson et al., Statistics for Business & Economics Ch. 8

lecture_notes.introduction: Confidence intervals provide a range of plausible values for a population parameter. They quantify the uncertainty inherent in using sample data to estimate population characteristics.
key_concepts: Point estimate vs. interval estimate
CI for mean: x-bar +/- z*(sigma/sqrt(n)) or t*(s/sqrt(n))
CI for proportion: p-hat +/- z*sqrt(p-hat(1-p-hat)/n)
Interpretation: 95% CI means 95 of 100 samples would produce intervals containing the true parameter
Sample size determination: n = (z*sigma/E)^2
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building confidence intervals from real survey data: calculating 90%, 95%, and 99% intervals and discussing the width-confidence tradeoff

review.value: A 95% CI does NOT mean 95% probability the parameter is in this specific interval; it means 95% of all possible samples would produce intervals that capture the true parameter
Half the width of the CI; decreases with larger sample size (proportional to 1/sqrt(n)) and increases with higher confidence level

learning_objectives: Formulate null and alternative hypotheses for business questions
Conduct hypothesis tests for means and proportions
Interpret p-values and understand Type I and Type II errors
readings: Anderson et al., Statistics for Business & Economics Ch. 9

lecture_notes.introduction: Hypothesis testing provides a rigorous framework for making decisions based on data. It answers the question: is the evidence strong enough to conclude that an effect or difference is real, not just random variation?
key_concepts: Null hypothesis (H0): status quo, no effect, no difference
Alternative hypothesis (Ha): the claim being tested
Test statistic: how far the sample result is from the null in standard error units
P-value: probability of observing the sample result (or more extreme) if H0 is true
Type I error (alpha): rejecting a true H0. Type II error (beta): failing to reject a false H0
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete hypothesis test: a company claims its new process reduces defect rate. Testing the claim with production data.

review.value: The probability of getting a test statistic as extreme as observed if the null hypothesis is true. Small p-value = strong evidence against H0.

review.key: Type I vs. Type II Error
review.value: Type I: concluding an effect exists when it does not (false positive). Type II: missing a real effect (false negative). There is a tradeoff: reducing one increases the other.

learning_objectives: Calculate and interpret the correlation coefficient
Build a simple linear regression model and interpret the output
Evaluate regression assumptions and model fit
readings: Anderson et al., Statistics for Business & Economics Ch. 14-15

lecture_notes.introduction: Regression analysis is the most widely used statistical technique in business. It allows us to model relationships between variables and make predictions.
key_concepts: Correlation coefficient (r): measures linear relationship strength and direction
Simple linear regression: y = b0 + b1*x + error
Least squares method: minimizes sum of squared residuals
R-squared: proportion of variance in y explained by the model
Regression assumptions: linearity, independence, normality, equal variance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a regression model predicting sales from advertising spend: estimating coefficients, interpreting output, checking assumptions, and making predictions

review.value: R-squared = 0.75 means 75% of the variation in y is explained by the regression model; the remaining 25% is unexplained variability
b1 represents the estimated change in y for a one-unit increase in x, holding other variables constant; both magnitude and sign matter

questions.question: Customer wait times at a bank are normally distributed with mean 8 minutes and standard deviation 2 minutes. What percentage of customers wait more than 12 minutes? What wait time represents the 90th percentile?
questions.answer: More than 12 minutes: Z = (12-8)/2 = 2.0. P(Z > 2.0) = 1 - 0.9772 = 0.0228 = 2.28%. 90th percentile: Z = 1.28 (from table). X = 8 + 1.28(2) = 10.56 minutes. So 90% of customers wait less than 10.56 minutes.

rubric.key: Correct Z-score and probability for >12 min

questions.question: A diagnostic test for a rare disease has a sensitivity of 95% (correctly detects disease when present) and specificity of 90% (correctly identifies no disease when absent). The disease prevalence is 1%. If a person tests positive, what is the probability they actually have the disease?
questions.answer: Using Bayes' theorem: P(Disease|Positive) = P(Positive|Disease) x P(Disease) / P(Positive). P(Positive) = P(Pos|Disease)P(Disease) + P(Pos|No Disease)P(No Disease) = 0.95(0.01) + 0.10(0.99) = 0.0095 + 0.099 = 0.1085. P(Disease|Positive) = 0.0095 / 0.1085 = 8.76%. Despite the positive test, there is only an 8.76% chance the person has the disease. This is because the disease is so rare that false positives vastly outnumber true positives.

questions.question: A sample of 64 customer transactions has a mean of $85 and standard deviation of $20. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the population mean transaction value and interpret it.
questions.answer: SE = s/sqrt(n) = 20/sqrt(64) = 20/8 = 2.5. For 95% CI with large sample, z* = 1.96. CI = 85 +/- 1.96(2.5) = 85 +/- 4.90 = ($80.10, $89.90). Interpretation: We are 95% confident that the true population mean transaction value lies between $80.10 and $89.90. If we repeated this sampling process many times, 95% of the resulting intervals would contain the true mean.

questions.question: A company claims their new packaging reduces breakage below the current 5% rate. A sample of 500 shipments shows 18 broken items (3.6%). At alpha = 0.05, test whether the new packaging significantly reduces breakage.
questions.answer: H0: p >= 0.05 (breakage rate is 5% or higher). Ha: p < 0.05 (breakage rate is lower, one-tailed). Test statistic: p-hat = 18/500 = 0.036. Z = (0.036 - 0.05) / sqrt(0.05 x 0.95 / 500) = -0.014 / sqrt(0.000095) = -0.014 / 0.00975 = -1.44. P-value = P(Z < -1.44) = 0.0749. Since p-value (0.0749) > alpha (0.05), we fail to reject H0. There is not sufficient evidence at the 5% level to conclude the new packaging reduces breakage below 5%. The observed reduction could be due to random variation.

major_assignments.title: Statistical Analysis of a Business Dataset
major_assignments.description: Obtain a real business dataset and conduct a comprehensive statistical analysis including descriptive statistics, confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and regression analysis. Present findings in a professional analytical report.
objectives: Apply descriptive statistics to summarize and visualize business data
Construct and interpret confidence intervals for key parameters
Conduct hypothesis tests to answer specific business questions
Build a regression model and interpret the results
deliverables: At least two hypothesis tests with full documentation
Regression analysis with interpretation and diagnostics

criteria: Confidence intervals correctly constructed and interpreted
Hypothesis tests properly formulated and conducted
Clear writing with correct statistical terminology

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of statistical concepts and calculations

problem_sets.description: Weekly quantitative problems covering all statistical techniques

major_assignments.description: Statistical analysis of a business dataset project

midterm_examination.description: Covers descriptive statistics, probability, and sampling distributions

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, and regression

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Statistical concepts build cumulatively; missing classes creates compounding gaps.
course_policies.late_submissions: Problem sets penalized 10% per day. Statistical analysis project has firm deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Problem sets may be discussed but must be submitted independently. Analysis project is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All calculations and analysis must be your own. Statistical software output must be from your own work, not copied.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Scientific calculator required. Statistical software: Excel with Data Analysis Toolpak, or R/Python. Access to datasets.
